description:
## Symptoms

Two related failure modes when an `if` expression contains a function
call in either branch.

### (A) Constant condition → panic at IR construction

```fz
fn helper(), do: 7
fn main() do
  if 1 == 0 do
    print(helper())
  else
    print(99)
  end
end
```

```
$ fz run repro_a.fz
thread 'main' panicked at src/fz_ir.rs:474:14:
unknown block
```

`block_mut` (src/fz_ir.rs:470) panics looking up a BlockId that was
never registered. Reachable via interp and jit subcommands alike, so
the failure is in IR lowering, not codegen.

### (B) Variable condition, monomorphizable callsites → silent output drop

```fz
fn helper(), do: 7
fn pick(n) do
  if n == 0 do helper() else 99 end
end
fn main() do
  print(pick(0))
  print(pick(1))
end
```

```
$ fz run repro_b.fz
warning[type/unreachable-arm]: the else branch is never reachable
  ...
  = note: this value here has type `0`
  = note: narrowing for this branch would need `none`,
          but that intersection is uninhabited (unreachable arm at bb5)

warning[type/unreachable-arm]: the then branch is never reachable
  ...
  = note: this value here has type `1`
  = note: narrowing for this branch would need `none`,
          but that intersection is uninhabited (unreachable arm at bb3)

$ echo $?
0
```

Exits 0 with no stdout. The two `print` calls produce nothing. Expected
output is `7` then `99`.

## Common factor

A call expression sits inside an `if`/`else` arm in expression position.
Per-callsite narrowing prunes one arm to `none` (uninhabited). Two
divergent outcomes from there:

- The constant-cond shape reaches a code path that asks `block_mut` for
  the pruned arm's block — that BlockId was never registered, so the
  lookup panics.
- The variable-cond shape monomorphizes per callsite so each
  monomorphization sees *one* arm as dead; the surviving arm should
  hold the live value, but the call's result never makes it back to the
  caller — `print` receives nothing observable and exits 0.

The shared upstream cause is almost certainly arm-pruning in
ir_lower (or wherever the typer narrowing meets block emission)
not stitching the surviving arm's value back into the join properly,
and forgetting to register or rewire the dead arm's block.

## Reproduction notes

- Discovered while drafting a fixture (`fixtures/actor_ring/`, since
  reverted) that used `if n == 0 do send(...) else spawn(...) ; send(...) end`.
- Without a function call inside the arms (e.g. plain literals) neither
  failure mode triggers.
- Wrapping `receive()` in a helper does NOT work around it — the helper
  call is itself the trigger (case A above).
- Multi-clause dispatch (`fn relay(0, ...) do ... end / fn relay(n, ...)`)
  reaches the same panic when one clause body holds the call shape — so
  the bug should be filed against the lowering of branching constructs
  in general, not `if` specifically.

## Suggested first probe

1. Add a debug dump of the BlockId set right before the panicking
   `block_mut` call (src/fz_ir.rs:474) — find out *which* block id is
   missing and which builder call should have registered it.
2. Cross-check ir_lower's `if` arm path against the typer's
   per-callsite narrowing: when one arm is pruned to `none`, what's
   supposed to happen to its BlockId allocation and to the join's
   incoming edges?
3. Add the two reproducers as fixture-style tests under `tests/` so
   the panic is captured by `cargo test` going forward.

## Out of scope for this ticket

- Fixing `fixtures/actor_ring/`. That fixture is blocked on this bug
  and will be re-added once the lowering is fixed.
- Anything about the typer's narrowing decision itself — that may be
  correct; the bug is downstream in how the lowering handles a pruned
  arm.

comments.text: Fixed by fz-duq (.2 covers both repros; .5 graduates them to fixtures). The third no-narrowing variant (repro C) is also covered. The actor_ring fixture mentioned in this ticket is still blocked on the multi-clause class of the same bug, tracked separately as fz-g8v.

title: if-branch with fn call panics IR construction; variable cond silently drops output
